back_translation: Faith is the assurance in things that we hope for, and is the proof of things that we cannot see.
For by this faith, our forefathers received commendation.
By faith, we understand that the universe was made by the divine word of God, in order that what we can see did not come from things that have appeared.

By faith, Abel offered to God a sacrifice better than that of Cain. By this sacrifice, he received commendation as a righteous person, when God acknowledged his offerings; and by this same faith, although he is dead, he still speaks.

By faith, Enoch was taken up so that he would not have to see death; and no one could find him because God had taken him away. For before he was taken up, there was a commendation that he was pleasing to God.
And without faith, it is impossible to be pleasing to God; for the one who comes before God must believe that He exists, and that He is the giver of reward to those who earnestly seek Him.

By faith, Noah, when he received the warning concerning things not yet seen, with godly fear built a great boat to save his family; through this faith he condemned the world, and became an heir of the righteousness that comes by faith.
By faith, Abraham, when he was called to go out to a place that he would later receive as inheritance, obeyed; and he set out without knowing where he was going.
By faith, he sojourned in the land of the promise as a foreigner; he dwelt in tents together with Isaac and Jacob, who were fellow-heirs of the same promise.
For he was waiting for the city that has foundations, whose architect and builder is God.
By faith, Sarah herself received the power to conceive, even though she was past the proper age, because she considered the One who promised to be faithful.

Therefore, from one man, who had become as good as dead, there came forth a multitude of descendants, like the stars in the sky and like the grains of sand at the edge of the sea, which cannot be counted.

All these persons died in faith, having not received the things that had been promised, but having seen and welcomed those things from afar; and they confessed that they were merely strangers and sojourners on the earth.
For those who say things like this clearly show that they are seeking their own homeland.
And if they had thought of the homeland from which they came out, they would have had the opportunity to return.
But now, they are longing for a homeland that is better, namely a heavenly one. Therefore, God is not ashamed to allow Himself to be called their God, because He has prepared a city for them.
By faith, Abraham, when he was being tested, offered up Isaac. The one who had received the promises was offering his only son,
even though God had spoken to him, 'Through Isaac, your offspring will be named.'
He reasoned that God was able to raise the dead, and so he received Isaac back in a figurative sense.
By faith, Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau concerning things that would come in the future.
By faith, Jacob, when he was about to die, blessed each of Joseph's sons, and worshiped while leaning on the top of his staff.
By faith, Joseph, when he was near to death, remembered the exodus of the descendants of Israel, and gave instructions concerning his bones.
By faith, when Moses was born, his parents hid him for three months, because they saw that the child was unusually beautiful, and they were not afraid of the king's edict.
By faith, Moses, when he had grown up, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter.
He chose to suffer affliction together with the people of God, rather than to enjoy the pleasures of sin only for a brief time.
He considered the reproach for the sake of Christ to be wealth greater than the treasures of Egypt, because he was looking ahead to the reward.
By faith, he abandoned Egypt, not fearing the wrath of the king, because he endured as if he could see the One who exists invisibly.
By faith, he established the festival of Passover and the sprinkling of blood, in order that the destroyer of the firstborn would not touch the descendants of Israel.
By faith, the people crossed the Red Sea as if walking on dry ground, but when the Egyptians attempted to do the same, they sank and disappeared.
By faith, the walls of the city of Jericho fell down, after they had marched around those walls for seven days.
By faith, Rahab the prostitute did not perish along with those who were disobedient, because she received the spies in peace.
And what more shall I say? For there will not be enough time for me to recount the stories of Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, and the prophets,
who by faith conquered kingdoms, did things in righteousness, received what had been promised, shut the mouths of lions,
quenched the power of fire, escaped the edge of the sword, received strengthening from weakness, became strong in war, and made the armies of foreigners retreat.
Women received their dead back through resurrection. And others were tortured to death on the rack, refusing to accept release, so that they might receive a better resurrection.
And still others suffered with mocking and flogging; moreover with chains and being imprisoned.
They were stoned, they were sawn with a saw, they were killed with a sword. They wandered around clothed in sheepskins and goatskins, in want, oppressed, and mistreated.
The world was not worthy of these persons. They wandered in deserts and among mountains, in caves, and in holes in the ground.
And all of these persons, although they received commendation by faith, did not receive what had been promised.
Because God had prepared something better for us, in order that they would not receive completion-perfection without us.
